does: This paper introduces a fraud detection layer designed to identify adversarial interaction patterns in LLM-powered agents by analyzing sequences of interactions rather than individual prompts. The core contribution is a runtime monitoring system that extracts structured features from interaction trajectories including prompt characteristics, session dynamics, tool usage patterns, and execution context to detect emerging risks. The approach uses lightweight machine learning models to enable low-latency real-time detection, moving beyond traditional prompt-level filtering and rule-based guardrails that fail to capture risks that accumulate gradually across multi-turn conversations.
connects: This work builds on research into prompt injection attacks and adversarial prompting against language models, extending beyond existing defenses that typically operate at the prompt level. It connects to broader work on agent safety and autonomous system monitoring, drawing inspiration from fraud detection literature which has long dealt with detecting patterns across sequences of transactions. The paper addresses limitations of current guardrail approaches by recognizing that sophisticated attacks exploit multi-turn interactions and gradual escalation strategies, echoing concerns in adversarial robustness and security research about sequential attack capabilities.
enables: This detection framework enables more robust deployment of autonomous LLM agents in high-stakes applications by providing complementary security monitoring alongside content filtering. The trajectory-based approach opens research directions in understanding how adversarial strategies evolve across conversations and how to design interaction-level defenses. It potentially enables new safety architectures where agents are continuously monitored for behavioral anomalies, and facilitates development of better attack simulation methods for testing agent vulnerability to multi-turn manipulation, supporting the broader goal of safe autonomous agent deployment.
